Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, in the first year of his reign, took Jerusalem, and carried away whoever and whatever he pleased. Most people think that the seventy years of captivity began with this event. It is beneficial for rulers to employ wise men; and it is wise for them to discover and train such individuals. Nebuchadnezzar ordered that these chosen youths should be taught. All their Hebrew names had something of God in them, but to make them forget the God of their fathers, the guide of their youth, the pagans gave them names that suggested idolatry. It is troubling to consider how often public education tends to corrupt principles and morals.

The benefit we think we gain for ourselves, we must acknowledge as God's gift. Daniel remained steadfast in his religion. Whatever they called him, he still held fast the spirit of an Israelite. These youths were concerned about the food, lest it be sinful. When God’s people are in Babylon, they need to take special care not to participate in her sins. It is commendable for young people not to desire or seek sensual pleasures. Those who want to excel in wisdom and piety must learn early to control their bodies. Daniel avoided defiling himself with sin; and we should fear that more than any external trouble. It is easier to keep temptation away, than to resist it when it is near. And we cannot better improve our standing with those who have shown us favor, than to use it to keep us from sin. People will not believe the benefit of avoiding excess and of a simple diet, nor how much they contribute to the health of the body, unless they try. Conscientious temperance will always do more, even for comfort in this life, than sinful indulgence.

Daniel and his companions remained faithful to their religion; and God rewarded them with excellence in learning. Pious young people should strive to do better than their peers in useful things; not for the praise of humans, but for the honor of the gospel, and so that they may be prepared for service. And it is good for a country, and for the honor of a ruler, when he can judge who are best suited to serve him, and prefers them because of that. Let young men consistently study this chapter; and let all remember that God will honor those who honor him, but those who despise him will be lightly regarded.
